phrasalVerbs.definition: To have a friendly relationship with someone.
examples: Do you get along with your new colleagues?
My sister and I get along really well.
It's hard to get along with people who are always negative.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To have an argument and stop being friendly with someone.
examples: They fell out over a misunderstanding about money.
I fell out with my best friend last year, but we made up.
It's common for siblings to fall out when they're young.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become friendly with someone again after an argument.
examples: They argued on Monday but made up by Wednesday.
It's important to make up after a fight.
Have you made up with your brother yet?
They broke up after three years together.
She's upset because she just broke up with her boyfriend.
It's never easy to break up with someone you care about.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To take care of someone or something.
examples: Can you look after my cat while I'm on holiday?
She looks after her elderly parents every weekend.
It's important to look after your mental health.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To develop from a child to an adult; figuratively, to behave maturely.
examples: She grew up in a small town in the south.
Oh, grow up! Stop arguing about such a small thing.

fillInExercises.sentence: Do you ___ well with your new flatmates?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get along' means to have a friendly relationship.

fillInExercises.sentence: They ___ out over something silly and stopped talking for a week.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Fall out' (past: fell out) means to have an argument and stop being friends.

fillInExercises.sentence: After their big argument, it took them a week to ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Make up' means to become friends again after a disagreement.

fillInExercises.sentence: They ___ up after five years together — it was a shock to everyone.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Break up' (past: broke up) means to end a romantic relationship.

fillInExercises.sentence: Could you ___ my dog for a few days while I'm travelling?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Look after' means to take care of someone or something.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ up in a small village near the coast.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Grow up' (past: grew up) means to develop from child to adult.

fillInExercises.sentence: He always ___ with everyone — he's so easygoing.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get along' means to have good relationships.

fillInExercises.sentence: Stop fighting and ___ up — you're both adults!
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Grow up' figuratively means to behave in a mature way.

lines.speaker: Chloe
lines.text: You look upset. Is everything OK?

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: Not really. I [fall out] with Sarah over something stupid.

lines.speaker: Chloe
lines.text: You two usually [get along] so well. Have you tried to [make up]?

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: I tried, but she's not responding. I don't know how to [deal with] it.

lines.speaker: Chloe
lines.text: You need to [look after] yourself first — you look really stressed.

lines.speaker: Mia
lines.text: You're right. We just need to [grow up] and stop fighting over small things.

lines.speaker: Jake
lines.text: Did you hear? Tom and Lisa [break up] last week.

lines.speaker: Emily
lines.text: Really? They always seemed to [get along] so well. What happened?

lines.speaker: Jake
lines.text: I heard they [fall out] about where to live after university.

lines.speaker: Emily
lines.text: That's sad. I hope they [make up] eventually — they were close friends.

lines.speaker: Jake
lines.text: Lisa [look after] Tom a lot when he was ill. It'd be a shame to lose that.

lines.speaker: Emily
lines.text: Sometimes people just need time to [grow up] and see what matters.
